speaker: interviewer
text: We were talking about the ghetto. What did your family take with you into the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we didn't take much with us. The-- the speed-- first of all, the-- the-- we were supposed to leave for Palestine at almost the same time when the war started. So we already had everything packed up, our things, and shipped to Palestine. It was already on the way going. I don't know where it wound up, where it is, or where it was at the time. Nobody knew. The war started. Everything was upside down. People left people, lost people. It was a-- it was really a way-- it was a mess. A few things that-- that we took with us, some cooking utensils and-- and-- and-- you just had a pot with you that you could go to the bathroom. We didn't have bathrooms. You must understand there were four families or three families in one of those big rooms here. And there was just a divider that was a sheet. And so it was the-- the-- it wasn't so nice as you have it here in a normal life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any running water?

speaker: interviewee
text: Running water, yes. We did have running water. And we also had-- the guys came by with water and they sold water. There was supposed to be a different water. But I think that we used our water quite a bit from the crane, from the-- you know, from what-- what was running in the house. And each one that had some certain amount of water. But it was not enough. They still came by with water that was maybe cleaner or better treated. And they sold it to us on a daily basis.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what you took personally to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I cannot remember. But whatever it was just what I had done. There was really nothing that you can-- you couldn't go to a store and say, I'm going to buy some things because I'm going to be in-- in the ghetto. It was an order that in 15 minutes, you better be in the ghetto, or 20 minutes or an hour or 2 hours. It was a push. And at that time, a lot of people died. And they-- they would kill people if they don't show up there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you witness any of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Quite a bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Quite a bit. Well, you know, the Germans were very efficient in-- in taking care of killing people. So it was there the idea was that they are cleaning up an area. When they said, OK, this street, this street, from 1 to 12, everything has to be cleared out, they were-- they were very efficient in that. And they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it wasn't a matter-- they didn't give you enough choice to make up your mind whether you want to go in the ghetto. They want that you be there and you better be there, period. Otherwise, you get shot. So it was-- it was organized. They organized it well. Let's put it this way, because we couldn't help it. That's the way it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: And everybody was-- wanted to live through together. Everybody went. We were hoping that maybe in six weeks, eight weeks, two months, three months, the war will be ended. They-- nobody would believe that Germany was able to do all these things. Nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were hoping that it's only a matter of just a few weeks. It wasn't. It got worse and worse and worse. Were you able to continue your studies at all in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I-- I was not. I was not because I had to-- I had to get a job. First of all, you had to have a job. Otherwise they right away selected you to be shipped out of the ghetto. And when they shipped you out of the ghetto, you were finished. They'll take you out in the forest and shoot you and-- or get rid of you.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know that at that-- at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't. That was the thing, you know. They would always make up stories and tell us, oh, you are going to Germany and you are going to-- because you are 15 years old. And-- and you can get a job. And you will have it so much better and you will have enough bread to eat and-- and a house to live and all this. But it wasn't true. They just took us out to-- they kept on killing people just about maybe five, six miles outside of the city, outside of the ghetto. So-- but people-- you know, people knew what was going on. Some people knew what was going on. And they-- they risked their lives and they would try to run away. They would they join some kind of organizations that would fight. You know, there were some partisans and stuff like this. These were the people that were a little older than I, see. And they took the chances. But a lot of people who had a sister, a mother, they had to-- they worry about and thinking, well, maybe in three months, everything will be over, you wouldn't go ahead and risk your whole life. So therefore, I-- I was trying to help get something to eat, something to-- some wood or some coals or-- you know, Poland is very cold. And we were freezing. People were freezing. Every day you could walk the streets, you see people laying in the gutters dead. Thousands and thousands and thousands of people died there in the ghetto in the few years that we were in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a source of heat in your living arrangements?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The source of heat was you-- two times or three times a week you got some briquettes. So you had to go and stay in line, get briquettes. So this was the-- the amount. And some people, who-- who could buy black market, they sold everything off. They-- they would have diamonds or gold or whatever they had, they would sell just to get some of these things, to be able to get enough food, to get enough coals and stuff like this, to be able to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family have anything with you like that to sell?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I cannot say that. It was strictly a matter of [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] whatever-- you see, it is funny. When I look back today, I say a room like this here had maybe two, three families or four families in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't even think it was that big. When you come to think of, if you see it today, say, my god, how were here three families? That is impossible. How could that happen? It did happen. So again, each one had-- in the ghetto, each one was still able to maneuver things, you know. You had a job. You had a good job. Sometimes they would give you a little extra food. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Money, if someone had some money was able to buy on black market and so forth. Was it Polish currency?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And then it was currency that was ghetto currency. I have that also, a few pieces still. It was ghetto currency. And that was-- one day, they said give me all your Polish money and here you have ghetto money. And so that's what happens-- happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your mother and sister adjust to all this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my sister became ill. She had a-- a lung disease on one lung. And they-- the doctors that-- she went to-- to the hospital. And they disconnected one of the lungs. So it-- she was still alive a few years. And then she got to Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: My mother-- we were just losing weight, you know? If somebody was 150 pounds, two years later, it was either she was already gone or she weighed-- she weighed 90 pounds or 70 pounds. You see, people were disintegrating slowly. And so there wasn't a matter of thinking, well, what am I going to do tomorrow? Am I going to eat enough to survive? You were trying to eat as much to survive. But not always did you have it. You mentioned your sister going to the hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had that happened before you entered the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That was already in the ghetto. She-- she-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] they-- they discovered that she had something with her lung. And they-- they disconnected-- they did some kind of operation and they put that lung to rest so it didn't develop any more sicknesses and things like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you visit her in the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't think so. It was just a couple of days or something like that. And then she was let out. And she was-- and she was home, laying at home, and-- and tried to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about-- did you have a job in the ghetto? How did go about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --getting a job?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had some work. First I was working-- I-- I had three or four jobs in the ghetto, and always tried to improve myself where there was more food available. Once I got a job to work in a factory where they made straw shoes, shoes out of straw. And they send those off to the Russian-- to Russia, because the Germans were freezing there, so they would put straw shoes over their regular uniform shoes. And that's what kept-- kept them warm, their feet warm, anyways. I was working there for a while. Then I was working in a-- in a-- in a kitchen. We just make soup, you know. All day long I would go mix and the-- the big pots what they had built in, huge things. And it was like potato soup or whatever. Then I was working as a-- as a-- they send me to do things. I was a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] well, some of the jobs, they don't even have here that they would create over there because of the circumstances. I would run from one office to the other, take papers here, bring them over there, bring it-- this was one of the jobs it was a better job, because I was able to walk out and speak to people and see new people and so forth. Then I was working for a-- and that was one of the better jobs I had was a office that was like a controlling office would-- which would control some of the thieveries going on, things like this, things that-- if something was going on in a grocery, somebody would steal, these people would go there and make sure that it's clean, that it's cleaned up, or they would do the right thing. It was called [NON-ENGLISH]. It was-- that was a good job, because Rumkowski, the president of the ghetto, would come there once a week. And he would-- we would have-- he would have a conference there. And I was able to put his coat on and-- and say hello to him. And he would always, you know, push my hair. And it was the-- a better job for me, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned working in the kitchen and cooking soup. Who was that soup for?

speaker: interviewee
text: The soup was for people that came there to eat soup every 12:00-- between 12:00 and 1:00 or 12:00 and 3:00, people from these various [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] shops that had to do work came to eat their-- their lunch. So it was just soup, potato soup.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to have any extra food through working there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was good, because, you know, the Germans would give us sometimes some bones for the soup and stuff like this, so we were able to kind of sneak-- sneak a little bit for ourselves, I mean steal a little bit for ourselves, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any religious activities in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I do not. As I said before, not-- I was not involved in any of the religious activities in the ghetto, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about any sort of entertainment or cultural activities?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were things going on. But you must understand I was the age-- my age was a very-- not the very best age to be in a ghetto in a-- in any kind of concentration camp, because when I came out, I learned nothing and I was 20 years old, where most people, if they were three, four years older than I was, they were older, they had more education to start off with. They had some high school education.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was-- I had some friends which we went out and we would go and say there's a speaker. But whatever he said was-- he couldn't speak the truth, because they would shoot him. So he would tell us a story. And in some cases, some schools were continuing in Lodz that you had maybe a few hours of learning. Some children from the ghetto went to school in Lodz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Yes. I did not receive any-- any school in the ghetto. I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anything about the government of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There was a Rumkowski. He was the president of the ghetto, Chaim Rumkowski. He was a-- he had an office in middle of the ghetto. And he had a staff. He had secretaries. He had people that worked. He had a police department and a police chief and a fire department and a fire chief. And-- and they had-- they had organized a little country. You know, it was like a little enterprise. And they would run the ghetto. They were the policemen. They were the firemen. They were the-- the people that run the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of groups of people, transports, arriving to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You talked a little bit already about them leaving.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you talk about that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We had groups coming in from Czechoslovakia, from all-- all the-- you see, what the Germans did, as the ghettos were-- started to get organized, they saw that the ghetto in Lodz is very well organized. So they would liquidate the smaller ghettos. They would kill-- if there were 2,000 people, let's say, well, they would maybe kill 1,000 or 1,500, and take the 500 and send them in into the Lodz ghetto. The biggest experience and the most-- the most outstanding experience that I had were the people from Czechoslovakia when they came in. They were all very well fed. They had beautiful clothes. They didn't know that there is such a thing, a ghetto, or something like what happened in Poland. And they came in in the-- in-- after two, three years after the Lodz ghetto was organized. And they came in and six weeks later, they died there. They could not-- at that point, to the ghetto was already-- you know, as time went on, we got less and less and less food. When the ghetto started, ah, it's not so bad. Nobody was really thinking of doing anything, running away or anything like this. But as time went on, instead of having three people would have a bread, then it would be four people and five people and six people and seven people would get a bread. So it was less and less and less food, and people died faster too because of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would happen when people died? What would happen to their bodies?

speaker: interviewee
text: They would take them out-- in the ghetto, they would take them out. There were some cemeteries around there and they would bury them there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any of your relatives die in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, quite a few. But as I say, being that we had a-- quite a large family supposedly, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you had no way to go from here to the other side-- end of the ghetto. You didn't even know whether he is alive or dead. So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was not-- it was not a way to-- to really analyze. I don't think that there were smart enough people in there to really know exactly what was going on, unless they had some kind of connections to the Germans who would tell him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me some of the names of your relatives who died in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, gosh. That would be-- it would be really a-- it would be hard. But I had-- my mother had relatives that died in the ghetto. Some of them went to Warsaw. You see, Warsaw, everybody was hoping that in Warsaw they are not going to kill the people like in Lodz, because Warsaw was not in the-- in the Third Reich, so it was the [NON-ENGLISH] they called it. And they said they're not going to kill people that are not in the Third Reich. Lodz was in the Third Reich, so they said, well, ultimately, they're gonna to kill everybody in there, whereas in Warsaw, they will not do that. Little did we-- anybody knew that what was going in Warsaw, that most people died there. There was very few-- very few left over.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about some of the later deportations from the Lodz ghetto? Did the type of deportations change--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --from early to later?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Once they started going in a-- in a bigger speed and more-- more active, you know, liquidation of the ghetto, they would go regularly, you know. They would take 1,000 people, 1,200 people. First they would have excuses why they take people who don't have husbands and have children, and they said they cannot support them, so they have to send them out to another ghetto. They didn't tell them that they're sending them to Auschwitz or they're taking them outside and shoot them. They did not tell anyone about it. They just say they-- they are taking them to a different place, that this has a different system, and over there they can make their life different. But all these people are dead. None of them came through alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a physical boundary around the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There was-- there were-- there was a-- a fence. There were fences. And in the ghetto, through the ghetto was a-- a regular trolley car going, which had non-Jewish people. You know, in the city of Lodz, they had to go from one end to the city to the other. They would take the trolley car. Jews wouldn't take the trolley car there, that route, that particular one. But it was fenced around. And-- and in middle, where the trolley car would go, they would have big staircase and-- for the Jewish people to go across and to the other side of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you see people going on the trolley car?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: See them through the window and they would see you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But it didn't mean anything. It didn't mean anything whether you see them, because these-- these were not Jewish people. They were Polish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the penalty for trying to leave or escape from the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you got shot or-- if they caught you, you were in trouble. You either got shot or you got-- I don't know. They eliminated you. There was-- there are very few that I have heard of that they ran away and are alive, very few. But some did. Some did and were successful. But it might be a handful of people, not enough to liquidate. I think-- I think if I'm not mistaken, the Lodz ghetto had originally something like 115,000 people. Imagine. That's a lot of people. That's an awful lot of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in the ghetto altogether?

speaker: interviewee
text: About four years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what age were you when you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I went to Auschwitz and I was at that time about 18 years old. 18.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- did you leave with your mother and sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We--

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how were you chosen to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they said, here, this block and this-- these streets will leave. We are going to Germany and they're gonna do all these good things for us. But we wound up in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you transported there?

speaker: interviewee
text: By-- by cattle car-- cattle trains. And, well, a day later, we were in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what time of the year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in August 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the conditions on the cattle car?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lot of people died right there. You know, they couldn't hold it. They couldn't-- they couldn't live. They couldn't exist. Some people just could not exist. And so-- but right there and then, there was the selection, right or left, right or left. The people who went to the left went out to the-- to the oven, to the gas chambers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you arrived at Auschwitz and the doors were opened, that's what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: All of a sudden we saw-- we came in there at night, and the lights and everything and the screaming and yelling and the [GERMAN] and-- and the dogs and everything. It was-- it was a crazy time. Absolutely crazy, crazy time that all of a sudden they hit us. And we didn't know how do you act, how do you-- how do you respond to it? But it was Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's what it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there-- then all of a sudden I didn't find my mother or sister. You know, each one they told you, make sure that you take your most serious things with you. Take some-- some food and-- and clothes. Take-- and over there, [GERMAN], no, you don't need this. You're going to the oven, just like that. It was just unbelievable. And they had a lot of the people that were in Auschwitz, they were the ones that did the work for the Germans. This was the kommando is what they called it, you know. And they were the ones, they-- they-- they-- if you had a watch, forget it. It was taken away. Everything was take away, with the exception of your belt for a man. That was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: After you arrived and they told you to go out which direction?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Mengele, the German. Mengele. And he was right or left, right or left. They cleared out-- I would say we had a transport maybe-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] maybe 800 people, maybe 500 or 500 people that arrived to Auschwitz that day. Well, 500, 550 went right away to the-- to the gas chambers. The rest went into the-- into the labor camp they called.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know it was Mengele?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we didn't-- I didn't know him before, you know. This all happened, you know, at the time getting off the-- from the train into this and being selected. And before you know it, when you went into the camp, they put you in the showers and the-- and all that. We found out later on that it was Mengele, see. So-- and he had his staff. He had this German SS and stuff like this, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you tattooed at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The-- I didn't get tattooed, because they-- the same day as I came into the ghetto, they had-- a day before they tattooed the people in that camp. But they gave me a number.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your number?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did that represent to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. They-- we thought, you know, we-- instead of a name, it will be easier for them to know the-- the number. So it didn't mean anything. It was only that if you had to report to-- to the office in-- one of the German camp leaders where we were in the divisions, we had to say I'm Harry Guterman, Hersz Guterman, 95 [GERMAN], years, you know. And it was part of your identification but not-- nothing more.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live in Auschwitz? What were your living conditions like?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Auschwitz, we-- they put us in-- into the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Gypsy camp. A day before, they killed all the Gypsies out of that camp. They took them all into the gas chamber. It was called the [NON-ENGLISH]. And that was it. We got in. There was blood even on some of the walls in there, on the-- because some-- some of them we couldn't-- they couldn't clean up or whatever it was. They just-- that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's pause for a tape change, please.
